name: pywebview (Python Desktop Apps with Web Technologies)

performance.notes: Native WebView rendering; JavaScript bridge adds ~5ms; varies by platform WebView version

agent_readiness.notes:
Lightweight wrapper around native WebView for Python desktop apps. import webview. window = webview.create_window('App', 'https://example.com'). webview.start(). Load local HTML: webview.create_window('App', html='

Hello

'). JS-Python bridge: window.expose(my_python_func). From Python: window.evaluate_js('document.title'). From JS: window.pywebview.api.my_func(). Two-way JS↔Python communication. File dialog: window.create_file_dialog(). Native menus. Platforms: macOS (WebKit), Windows (WebView2), Linux (GTK WebKit). Electron alternative for Python devs.

gotchas: WebView version varies by OS — different rendering behavior on different machines
JavaScript bridge is synchronous — can block UI if misused
Windows requires WebView2 runtime (ships with Edge)
Not suitable for complex native UI interactions beyond web rendering
best_when: Packaging Python app with HTML/CSS/JS frontend as native desktop app
Electron-like desktop apps without Node.js dependency
Python FastAPI/Flask backends with web UI wrapped as desktop app
Cross-platform apps where web technologies handle UI complexity
avoid_when: When consistent rendering across OS WebViews is critical
summary: pywebview wraps native platform WebViews (WebKit/WebView2) for Python desktop apps. webview.create_window('App', html=html_content); webview.start(). Two-way Python↔JS bridge: window.expose(func) / window.evaluate_js(). Native menus and file dialogs. Cross-platform (macOS WebKit, Windows WebView2, Linux GTK). BSD-3. Electron alternative for Python devs. Best for HTML/CSS/JS frontend wrapped in a native desktop window with Python backend.
